mappings.rationaleAI: Stoicism from Zeno through Chrysippus, Epictetus, and Marcus Aurelius directly covers the school of Stoicism (A.1.g).
Epicureanism from Epicurus through Lucretius directly covers the school of Epicureanism (A.1.h).
Scepticism from Pyrrho through Sextus Empiricus directly covers the school of Skepticism (A.1.i).
Materialism from ancient atomism through dialectical materialism covers Atomism (A.1.d), Materialism (A.2.d), and views concerning mind-body relations (B.4).
Empiricism from Locke through Hume directly covers the school of Empiricism (A.2.b) and views of knowledge sources (C.3).
Popper's falsifiability and critical rationalism address Positivism and Logical Empiricism (A.2.i) and conceptions of knowledge validity (C.2).
Aquinas's Scholastic synthesis covers Scholasticism (A.1.j) and Thomism as a metaphysical theory (B.1).
Spinoza's pantheistic identification of God with Nature addresses Rationalism (A.2.c) and pantheism as a view concerning God's existence (B.2).
Logical Positivism and the Vienna Circle directly cover Positivism and Logical Empiricism (A.2.i).
Ordinary Language Philosophy with Austin, Ryle, and the later Wittgenstein directly covers Analytic and Linguistic philosophy (A.2.o).
Phenomenology from Husserl through Heidegger and Merleau-Ponty directly covers the school of Phenomenology (A.2.m).
Pythagoras and the Pythagorean tradition directly cover Pythagoreanism (A.1.a).
Neoplatonism from Plotinus through Proclus relates to Platonism (A.1.e) and different types of metaphysical theory (B.1).
Zeno's paradoxes represent Eleaticism (A.1.c), challenging the coherence of motion and plurality from the Eleatic philosophical position.
Mathematical Platonism covers Plato's theory of Forms applied to mathematics, addressing Platonism (A.1.e) and metaphysical theories (B.1).
The mind-body problem covering dualism, materialism, functionalism, and identity theory directly addresses views concerning mind-body relations (B.4).
The soul from Plato through Descartes to neuroscience covers views concerning the existence of mind and its relation to body (B.4).
Truth theories from correspondence to pragmatist views address different conceptions of the validity of knowledge (C.2).
Descartes' cogito and mind-body dualism directly cover Cartesianism (A.2.a) and dualism in mind-body relations (B.4).
Russell's contributions to analytic philosophy including logical atomism and the theory of descriptions address Analytic philosophy (A.2.o).
Berkeley's immaterialist idealism covers Empiricism (A.2.b), immaterialism in mind-body views (B.4), and conceptions of the object of knowledge (C.1).
Happiness from eudaimonia through utilitarianism addresses deontological and consequentialist theories of conduct (D.2).
Relativism from Protagoras addresses the Sophists (A.1.b) and different conceptions of knowledge validity (C.2).

China's Warring States period with Confucianism, Daoism, Legalism, and Mohism is relevant to the Sophists parallel in Chinese philosophy -- though this section focuses on Western schools, the outline does not include a non-Western section here. Reject.
The ontological argument from Anselm through Descartes and Plantinga addresses Scholasticism (A.1.j) and views concerning the existence of God (B.2).
Deism as a view concerning God's existence directly addresses different views concerning Deism (B.2).
Utilitarianism from Bentham through Mill and Singer directly covers the school of Utilitarianism (A.2.h).
The history of formal logic from Aristotle through Frege and Russell addresses Aristotelianism (A.1.f) in its treatment of Aristotelian logic and its subsequent development.
